1:
The chapter opens on a synchronism that the witnesses immediately test for accuracy: in the second year of Joash king of Israel, Amaziah the son of Joash began to reign over Judah. The reformed John Gill does the arithmetic and finds a wrinkle. Since Joash of Israel had begun to reign in the thirty-seventh year of Joash of Judah, who reigned forty years, Amaziah must properly have come to the throne in the fourth year of Joash of Israel; the "second year" therefore has to be understood of the second year after Joash of Israel reigned alone, for he had reigned two or three years in his father Jehoahaz's lifetime. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reaches the same solution from the other side, following Calmet: the "second year" points back to the time when Jehoahaz associated his son Joash with himself on the throne, so that this second year of Joash is really the first of his sole and independent government. The two schools converge on a co-regency; the apparent discrepancy dissolves once the overlap of reigns is allowed for.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts his eyes from the numbers to the man, and gives in advance the moral shape of the whole reign. He views Amaziah in three settings. In the temple he acted, in some measure, well, like his father Joash, but not like David; he kept up his attendance on God's altars and his attention to God's word, yet "he began well, but did not persevere." It is not enough, Henry warns, to do what our pious predecessors did merely to keep up the usage; we must do it as they did it, from the same principle of faith and devotion and with the same sincerity and resolution. On the bench he did justice, and in the field he triumphed, a fuller account of which the later verses will unfold. Henry's judgment is generous but guarded: a king who does right and does not endure in it has begun a course the historian will watch narrowly.

The witnesses do not divide here; they set the frame together. Gill and Clarke secure the chronology by the same device of co-regency, differing only in which end of the overlap they emphasize, while Henry supplies the character-sketch that the annals will fill in, a king outwardly conformed to the worship of the LORD but never wholly given to it, whose promising beginning bends, by the chapter's end, toward pride, defeat, and a violent death. The larger record of the reign, as Henry notes and the source itself flags, is told at greater length in 2 Chronicles 25, to which the reader is pointed for the acts here only summarized.
2:
The formal accession notice fills in Amaziah's age, the length of his reign, and his mother's name, twenty-five years old at his coming to the throne, twenty-nine years reigning in Jerusalem, his mother Jehoaddan of that same city. The reformed John Gill uses the figure to fix the chronology already begun at v.1: of those twenty-nine years, fourteen were contemporary with Joash king of Israel, who reigned sixteen, and fifteen fell after Joash's death, a reckoning that will matter when the historian later notes how long Amaziah outlived his conqueror.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch look past the numbers to the quality of the reign the numbers measure. Amaziah reigned, they say, in the same theocratic spirit as his father Joash, yet "not like David his father." They anchor that qualification in the Chronicler's more exact wording at 2 Chronicles 25:2, where the king is said to have done right, but "not with a perfect heart." The difference is not trivial. Like his father before him, Amaziah began in outward conformity to the worship of the LORD but did not carry it through to the end; in the closing years of his reign he fell into idolatry, bowing before the very gods of Edom he had conquered, as the fuller narrative at 2 Chronicles 25:14 records. The two schools thus read the verse from complementary angles: Gill measures the reign, Keil and Delitzsch weigh it.

Both witnesses agree on the one blemish the verse itself hints at by omission and the next verse will state, that the high places were not taken away. Keil and Delitzsch tie this to the same pattern that had marked the father: a piety that touched the altar but left the entrenched irregular worship of the land untouched. There is a quiet warning in the parallel with David. To be measured against the ideal king and found merely "like Joash" is already to be found wanting; the standard is not a good beginning but a whole and undivided heart, and it is precisely there, the witnesses agree, that Amaziah will be shown to fail. The reign that opens with so promising a verdict, twenty-nine years, a right course, a conformity to his devout father, carries within it the fatal reservation that his heart was never wholly the LORD's, and the chapter will trace the outworking of that reservation into pride, apostasy, and ruin.
3:
The evaluation formula proper now falls: "he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, yet not like David his father: he did according to all things as Joash his father did." The witnesses fasten on the double qualification, right, yet not like David; right, but only as Joash was. The reformed John Gill trims the praise to its true measure: Amaziah did right "at least externally, and at the beginning of his reign." The two limits are decisive. The rightness was outward, a matter of public conformity rather than a whole heart, and it was temporary, belonging to the reign's first phase before the apostasy that would overtake its close.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the same point through the Chronicler's hand. Where Kings says "yet not like David his father," the parallel writer paraphrases the reference to Israel's ideal king as "yet not with a perfect heart" (see 2 Chronicles 25:2), the very gloss that exposes what the reservation means. To fall short of David is not to be openly wicked like the kings of the north; it is to serve God with a divided and unsteady devotion, correct in form but wanting in singleness. Ellicott lets the Chronicler interpret the historian, and the two together define the flaw that the reign will lay bare.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Keil and Delitzsch, reads the clause "according to all things as Joash his father did" as the key to the resemblance and its danger. Amaziah walked in his father's theocratic spirit, and that spirit had always been incomplete, a piety that maintained the temple and its altars but never purged the high places, and that could, at the last, collapse into open idolatry. The measuring of son against father, and father against David, builds a descending scale on which Amaziah stands better than Joash's northern contemporaries yet well below the standard the LORD sets. The witnesses do not divide. Gill's "externally, and at the beginning," Ellicott's "not with a perfect heart," and Keil and Delitzsch's "as Joash did" are three ways of saying one thing: that a right beginning is not the same as a right heart, and that the verdict of this verse, generous on its face, already contains the seed of the tragedy the chapter will unfold. What looks like commendation is, on the witnesses' reading, a carefully hedged commendation, and the hedges are the whole point.
4:
A single limiting word governs the verse: "Howbeit the high places were not taken away; as yet the people did sacrifice and burnt incense on the high places." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott begins with that word, noting that the same Hebrew particle was rendered "yet" in the previous verse; here "Only," or "save that," would be better still. It is the standing exception the historian attaches to nearly every commended king of Judah, a fixed reservation that qualifies the praise just given. The rightness of v.3 is real, but it stops short of the one reform that would have proved it whole.

The reformed John Gill presses the continuity of the failure. Though Amaziah did what was right before God, he did not take away the high places; and here Gill widens the blame beyond the king, for neither had his father Joash taken them away. The corruption is inherited, passed down intact from reign to reign, tolerated by devout kings who touched everything but this. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley supplies the reason such things persist, in a sentence that reaches past the verse to a general law of the moral life: "It is hard to get clear of those corruptions which by long usage have gained prescription." Long custom, Wesley observes, lends error a kind of legal right; what has always been done acquires the look of what may rightly be done, and even a well-meaning reformer flinches from disturbing it.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse gains weight from their agreement. The people, not merely the king, were implicated, "as yet the people did sacrifice and burnt incense on the high places," so that the fault was national and popular, a habit of worship too deeply rooted to be dislodged by a king whose own heart was less than perfect. Ellicott's grammatical "save that," Gill's genealogy of neglect, and Wesley's proverb of prescription together explain why a good beginning left this stone unturned: because the wrong had the sanction of age, because both throne and people were content with it, and because it takes more than outward conformity to root out what long usage has made to feel like right. The unremoved high places stand, in the witnesses' reading, as the visible token of the divided heart the reign will finally betray.
5:
With his throne secure, Amaziah turns to the unfinished business of blood: "as soon as the kingdom was confirmed in his hand, he slew his servants which had slain the king his father." Every witness notes the deliberate delay, and reads in it a lesson in the timing of justice. The reformed John Gill explains what had to change before the king could act. He waited until he was well settled on the throne and had a share in the people's affection, until the idolatry and murder of his father's reign had faded from the public mind, and until the friends of the conspirators had dwindled to few or none. Justice was right, but it needed a ripe hour.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke names the murderers and gives the political reason for the pause. Those wicked men, Jozachar and Jehozabad, who had killed Joash of Judah, the assassination recorded at 2 Kings 12:20, had considerable power and influence, so that the young king found it dangerous to bring them to account until he was assured of the loyalty of his other officers. When that loyalty was clear, he called them to justice and put them to death. The delay was not weakness but prudence: a premature stroke against powerful men might have cost the king his crown.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the sense of the opening clause and fixes the Hebrew. "As soon as the kingdom was confirmed" means as soon as Amaziah was firmly established on the throne, as soon as he felt his power secure, a situation Ellicott compares to Solomon's at the outset of his reign, when the kingdom was likewise "established in his hand" at 1 Kings 2:46. The verbs rendered "slew" and "slain," he adds, are literally "smote" and "smitten," the same root turned upon the killers that they had turned upon the king. The witnesses do not divide. Together they portray a ruler who honoured the law's demand that a murderer's blood be required, yet who executed that demand with a statesman's patience, striking surely by striking slowly, waiting until the deed could be done without provoking the very upheaval that had carried off his father. That the historian pauses to record it at all signals that the act was reckoned righteous: the throne cleansed itself of the guilt of royal blood, in the appointed way and at the appointed time.
6:
Here the king's justice meets its God-given limit, and the witnesses gather to praise his restraint: "the children of the murderers he slew not, according unto that which is written in the book of the law of Moses." The law cited is Deuteronomy 24:16, "The fathers shall not be put to death for the children, nor the children be put to death for the fathers; but every man shall be put to death for his own sin." The reformed John Gill reads the sparing of the children as an instance of Amaziah's clemency and goodness, and of his strict regard both to justice and to the law of God, the more admirable because he might reasonably have feared that these spared sons would one day rise to avenge their fathers' deaths.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the act against its cultural background to measure how remarkable it was. To slay the children along with the offending father, they observe, was commonly the custom in the East in ancient times, and may very frequently have been done in Israel as well; Amaziah's refusal to follow that usage was a deliberate submission to the written word over the reflex of royal vengeance. They note in passing a small textual matter, that the Chethib form of the verb is correct, the Keri an unnecessary alteration made to conform to the wording of the law, a detail that shows how closely the historian's language leans on the statute he cites.

The wesleyan-arminian voices land on the same admiration from the side of the king's motive. Adam Clarke says plainly that here Amaziah showed his conscientious regard for the law of Moses, and quotes the statute in full as the ground of the king's mercy. John Wesley draws out the courage the obedience required: in sparing the children, Amaziah "showed faith and courage, that he would obey this command of God, though it was hazardous to himself," such persons being all too likely to seek revenge for a father's death. The witnesses do not divide; they are unanimous that this is the reign's finest hour. Where custom, policy, and the instinct of self-preservation all urged a wider slaughter, the king bowed instead to the plain text of Scripture, trusting God with the risk of leaving his enemies' sons alive. It is the one place in the chapter where Amaziah is shown choosing the harder, higher road, obedience above advantage, and every witness marks it as such.
7:
The chapter's first military triumph is compressed into a single verse, "He slew of Edom in the valley of salt ten thousand, and took Selah by war, and called the name of it Joktheel," and the witnesses expand it from the fuller record in Chronicles. The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke tell the campaign in almost identical terms: Amaziah raised an army of three hundred thousand choice men of Judah and hired a further hundred thousand from Israel for a hundred talents of silver, but at the word of a prophet he dismissed the hired troops and went against Edom with his own men alone. He slew ten thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt and took another ten thousand alive, whom he cast headlong from the rock, the whole expedition set out at length, both note, in 2 Chronicles 25:5 and following. Clarke identifies the captured stronghold Selah with Petra, the rock-hewn city that gave its name to Arabia Petraea.

The grammatical-historical witnesses add geography and philology. Charles Ellicott renders the opening emphatically, "he it was that smote," locates the Valley of Salt in the El-Ghor, the salt plain south of the Dead Sea that Amaziah would traverse in marching against Edom (comparing 2 Samuel 8:13), and explains Selah as the Hebrew for "the crag," the famous rock city. The taking of the capital, he adds, implies the reduction of the whole country, so that the humiliating loss of Edom under Jehoram (2 Kings 8:20) was now avenged. Keil and Delitzsch, whose account runs on from the previous verse, stress the ominous sequel that Chronicles supplies: the dismissed Israelite mercenaries, enraged at being sent home, fell upon several cities of Judah on their way and put many to death, a wound that will fester into the war of the coming verses.

The witnesses differ, mildly, over the meaning of the new name Amaziah gave the conquered city. Ellicott offers two renderings of Joktheel, "God's ward," referring to the natural strength of its position, or "subjugated of God." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads it instead as "the obedience of God," a name given the place by God as a reward for the king's obedience to the prophet's message at 2 Chronicles 25:8. The divergence is only etymological, and it does not touch the substance on which all agree: that the victory was real, that it was won by dismissing human help at a prophet's word and trusting God alone, and that its very completeness, the slaughter, the precipitated captives, the seized capital, would feed the pride that undoes the king before the chapter is done. Ellicott adds the sombre note that the gods of Edom, carried home from this triumph, would prove a snare, turning the day of victory into the root of Amaziah's fall.
8:
Flushed with the conquest of Edom, Amaziah sends a reckless challenge to the north: "Come, let us look one another in the face." Every witness recognizes it as a formal declaration of war, and they search out its cause. The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agree on the immediate provocation. The hundred thousand Israelite mercenaries whom Amaziah had dismissed before the Edomite campaign had gone home enraged, and on their way fell upon the cities of Judah from Samaria to Beth-horon, slew three thousand men, and carried off much spoil, the outrage recorded at 2 Chronicles 25:10 and following. Returning victorious, and stung by this injury, Amaziah sent to avenge it; Gill adds that an old grudge for what Jehu, the grandfather of the Israelite king, had done to his ancestors may have mingled with the fresh grievance, together with a hope of reducing the ten tribes again to the house of David.

The grammatical-historical witnesses look deeper, to the pride behind the pretext. Keil and Delitzsch grant the outward reason, the hostile acts of the mercenaries, but find the inward ground in the arrogance that victory had bred: Amaziah had forgotten the LORD who gave him the triumph, had brought home the gods of Edom and worshipped them, and had threatened into silence the prophet who condemned it (see 2 Chronicles 25:14); in that proud reliance on his own power he flung down the gauntlet. Charles Ellicott, noting that this fuller narrative plainly comes from a different source than the brief notice before it, judges that Amaziah, intoxicated by his recent success, aimed at nothing less than the recovery of the Ten Tribes for the house of David, and cites Josephus for the letters said to have passed between the kings.

So the witnesses divide, gently, over the true spring of the challenge: whether it was revenge for the mercenaries' raids or a proud ambition to reunite the kingdom. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry holds both together and drives home the folly. Amaziah, he says, challenged Joash "upon no provocation, and without showing any cause of quarrel," and thereby showed himself proud, presumptuous, and prodigal of blood, since thousands on both sides must be sacrificed to his capricious humour; "a fool's lips thus enter into contention, and his mouth calleth for strokes." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees that the challenge sprang from self-confidence and a desire of advancing his own glory, and adds the sober proverb that "he that is fond either of fighting or going to law will probably be the first that repents it." The verse thus turns on a single moral hinge the witnesses all grasp: the victory God gave has curdled into the pride that God resists, and the challenge that seems a bid for glory is in truth the first step toward the king's humiliation.
9:
Jehoash answers pride not with a threat but with a fable, and the witnesses linger over its barbed brevity. By the returning messengers, the reformed John Gill notes, the king of Israel sent back this reply: "The thistle that was in Lebanon sent to the cedar that was in Lebanon, saying, Give thy daughter to my son to wife: and there passed by a wild beast that was in Lebanon, and trode down the thistle." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the key: the fable is not to be pressed literally, as though Amaziah were the thorn-bush and Jehoash the cedar point for point, but the thorn-bush setting itself on a level with the cedar is a figure of a proud man overrating his strength, and its suit to the cedar a wish that overleaps the bounds of his condition. They liken the whole to the older parable with which Jotham had once rebuked his fellow-citizens at Judges 9:8, and warn against the over-reading that Thenius had drawn from it.

On the meaning of the details the witnesses converge. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke decodes it plainly: the thistle is Amaziah, the cedar is Jehoash, the demand "give thy daughter to my son" represents Amaziah's requiring that a part of Israel's kingdom be handed over to Judah, and the wild beast that tramples the thistle is Jehoash and his enraged army, who will shortly discomfit Amaziah, pillage the temple, and break down the walls of Jerusalem. Charles Ellicott agrees that the request for the daughter probably hints at Amaziah's demand for the surrender of Israel, the "daughter" of Jehoash, to Judah, and notes the versions' varied rendering of the plant: "thistle" in the Septuagint and Vulgate, "blackthorn" in the Syriac, a bramble or briar in the Hebrew. He too sets the parable beside Jotham's at Judges 9:8-15, remarking that the disdainful tolerance with which the strong northern kingdom regarded the southern could hardly have found more forcible expression.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley gathers the sting into a sentence: by the thistle, a low, contemptible, yet troublesome shrub, Jehoash means Amaziah; by the cedar, himself, far stronger, and quite out of the other's reach, and by the trampling beast, his own soldiers, who will tread down Amaziah and his forces with as little effort as a wild animal crushes a weed underfoot. The witnesses do not divide. They read the fable as a masterpiece of contemptuous warning: an offer of kinship turned into an insult, a proud challenger reduced to a roadside thistle, and a prophecy of ruin folded into a proverb. Jehoash, the evil king, speaks here with more wisdom than the righteous one, and the wisdom is wasted, for Amaziah will not hear.
10:
The cedar's fable now turns to plain warning, and the witnesses weigh both its shrewdness and its insolence. "Thou hast indeed smitten Edom, and thine heart hath lifted thee up: glory of this, and tarry at home; for why shouldest thou meddle to thy hurt?" The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott unfolds the Hebrew: "thou hast indeed smitten" means thou hast thoroughly worsted Edom, gained a brilliant victory; "glory of this, and tarry at home" is literally "be honoured, and abide in thine own house," be content with the glory won, rest on thy laurels, and do not risk them; and "why shouldest thou meddle to thy hurt" is better "why shouldst thou provoke disaster," the verb being that of attacking, as at Deuteronomy 2:5. The counsel is sound, however proudly framed.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes on the diagnosis "thine heart hath lifted thee up." "See where the root of all sin lies," he writes; "it is in the heart, thence it flows." It is not providence, nor the event, nor the occasion that makes men proud or secure, but their own hearts; Amaziah was proud of the blow he had given to Edom, as if that had made him formidable to all mankind, and so deceived himself as those do who magnify their own performances and, on the strength of a little success, conclude themselves fit for anything. Henry hears in the cedar's closing question the wisdom of Proverbs 20:3, that it is an honour to cease from strife, while every fool will be meddling, and notes that many would have had honour enough if they had only known when they had enough. The reformed John Gill agrees that pride lay at the bottom of the whole message, and that pride goes before a fall.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses press the moral outcome. Adam Clarke finds Jehoash's reply full of insolent dignity, offering no amends for the base and cowardly ravages his army had done to the defenceless women, old men, and children of Judah while the fighting men were away in Edom; yet, he insists, though Amaziah's quarrel was just, he was justly routed, for the true reason lay with God, who delivered Judah into the enemy's hand because Amaziah had brought home the gods of Seir and bowed to them (2 Chronicles 25:14, 2 Chronicles 25:20). John Wesley reads the cedar's advice as sound to the last: content thyself with that glory, and let not thine ambition betray thee to ruin. Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the story forward, Amaziah "paid no attention to this warning," and the armies would meet at Beth-shemesh in v.11. The witnesses converge on the tragedy the verse frames: the wisest word Amaziah ever received came from an enemy's mouth, and his lifted-up heart would not let him heed it.
11:
The warning falls on deaf ears: "But Amaziah would not hear." The reformed John Gill reads the refusal as more than stubbornness, it was a judicial hardness of heart, brought on by pride and permitted by God's overruling providence, so that the king might be punished for the idolatry into which his Edomite victory had led him, the setting up of the gods of Seir. The deafness, in other words, is itself a judgment: a man given over to his pride cannot hear even the soundest counsel, and the LORD uses that very deafness to bring about the fall he has decreed.

The grammatical-historical witnesses fix the place and the manoeuvre. Charles Ellicott explains that "looked one another in the face" means the armies encountered one another and joined battle, and locates Beth-shemesh at the modern Ain-shems, north of which lies a great plain, now the Wady-es-Surar, where the clash probably fell out. He notes the strategy: by choosing this ground Jehoash proposed to attack Jerusalem from the west, just as Hazael the Syrian had intended in the days of Joash at 2 Kings 12:17. Keil and Delitzsch, whose narrative runs on from the previous verses, add that Beth-shemesh lay on the border of Judah and Dan (see Joshua 15:10), and that there Judah was smitten before Israel, so that every man fled to his home.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse's terse "would not hear" gathers up the whole tragedy of the reign. Amaziah had received, from an enemy's mouth, wisdom he would never have taken from a friend's; he had been shown his folly in a fable and warned to be content with the glory he had; and he turned from it all, driven by a heart the previous verse had already named as "lifted up." Gill's judicial hardness and Ellicott's military geography meet in a single picture: a proud king marching his people to a field of his own choosing, only to have it become the place of his defeat. The scene is set at Beth-shemesh not by accident but by the convergence of Amaziah's obstinacy and God's justice, and the next verses will record how quickly the boast of v.8, "let us look one another in the face," is answered in the only way pride is ever answered.
12:
The battle is over almost before it is joined: "And Judah was put to the worse before Israel; and they fled every man to their tents." The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley both reach for Josephus to describe the rout. Judah could not face the army of Israel, Gill says; a sudden fear and consternation seized them, and before ever they joined battle they turned their backs. Wesley repeats the same tradition, that when the armies were to engage, the men of Judah "were struck with such a terror that they did not strike a stroke, but every man made the best of his way." The proud challenger's host melts without a blow, its courage failing at the sight of the foe it had provoked.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording of the flight. The received text reads "to his tent," the margin "to his tents," and the sense, he explains, is simply that the beaten army disbanded and scattered, each man to his home, as regularly happened after a great defeat, the same idiom of collapse he compares with 2 Kings 8:21. Keil and Delitzsch, carrying the narrative straight on, note that Judah's flight is only the prelude: Jehoash would seize the fleeing king at Beth-shemesh and press on to Jerusalem itself, breaking down four hundred cubits of its wall before returning north with the spoils, the acts the next verses relate.

The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the defeat was total and, in a way, self-inflicted: an army that had marched out on its king's proud dare fled without resistance, undone less by Israel's strength than by its own terror and the withdrawn favour of God. There is a grim symmetry in it. Amaziah had said, "Come, let us look one another in the face"; now Judah cannot look Israel in the face at all, but shows its back and runs. The boast is answered by a rout, and the field the king chose becomes the ground of his capture. What the fable of the thistle had foretold in figure, a proud weed trampled by a passing beast, the plain of Beth-shemesh now enacts in fact, and the historian records it with the same economy the disaster deserved: Judah put to the worse, every man fled, the war decided in a single line.
13:
The proud challenge of v.8 receives its bitter fulfilment: "And Jehoash king of Israel took Amaziah king of Judah, the son of Jehoash the son of Ahaziah, at Bethshemesh, and came to Jerusalem, and brake down the wall of Jerusalem from the gate of Ephraim unto the corner gate, four hundred cubits." The reformed John Gill catches the irony with a dry stroke: now indeed the two kings "looked one another in the face," as Amaziah had demanded, "but Amaziah must look very silly," the boast answered by the humiliation of capture.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the fullest account and finds meaning in every clause. The careful specification of Amaziah's descent, "the son of Jehoash the son of Ahaziah," he takes, with Thenius, as a mark that this narrative was drawn from the annals of the northern kingdom, and as an emphasis on the gravity of the event, an emphasis the Hebrew word-order heightens by naming the captive king first. Jehoash brought his prisoner to Jerusalem and made a breach in the wall, four hundred cubits, about two hundred and twenty yards, from the gate of Ephraim, which lay on the north (also called the gate of Benjamin, answering to the modern Damascus gate), to the corner gate at the north-western angle. No resistance, Ellicott observes, appears to have been offered; Josephus adds that Amaziah, under threat of death, ordered the gates thrown open, and that Jehoash drove his chariot in triumph through the breach, though the historian judges that needless, the army being already routed and the king a captive. The demolition was an act of sheer insolence, a forcible proof that even Judah's strongest city was no match for the prowess of Ephraim, and, as Thenius suggested, perhaps a way of making room for the victor's triumphal entry.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley sees a point even in the abrupt rehearsal of Amaziah's pedigree, "the son of Joash, the son of Ahaziah," set down here, he suggests, because the king now smarted for the iniquity of his ancestors. Adam Clarke notes a loose end the text leaves untied: Amaziah plainly regained his liberty afterward, for he outlived Jehoash by fifteen years, but on what terms he was released we are not told. Keil and Delitzsch, whose account runs through these verses, confirm that Jehoash kept the captured king with him and then returned to Samaria laden with the treasures and with hostages, the fuller inventory of which the next verse supplies. The witnesses do not divide. Together they render the complete reversal of the reign's high moment: the conqueror of Edom is himself conquered, the king who dared his rival to a face-to-face meeting is dragged as a prisoner to see his own capital's wall thrown down, and the pride diagnosed at v.10 reaps, stone by fallen stone, exactly the hurt the cedar had warned him not to meddle with.
14:
The victor strips the holy city: "he took all the gold and silver, and all the vessels that were found in the house of the LORD, and in the treasures of the king's house, and hostages, and returned to Samaria." The reformed John Gill notes that the vessels plundered were those which, according to the parallel account, had been in the keeping of the family of Obededom, to whom their charge had fallen by lot. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears a hint of poverty in the phrase "that were found": it suggests, he thinks, that there was not much treasure left to carry off, the temple already drained by earlier troubles, as the like phrase implied at 2 Kings 13:18.

On the hostages the witnesses agree and enlarge. Ellicott renders the word literally as "sons of sureties," and reads Jehoash's policy as calculated clemency: having humbled Amaziah's pride, he left him in possession of his throne but took hostages as security for his future good behaviour, an act of measured mercy such as the Assyrian kings of Sargon's line would later record of themselves. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke traces the same word to its root, a pledge or security given for the performance of a promise, the sense he had explained at Genesis 38:17, and supposes that Amaziah gave some of his nobles or his own family as pledges in order to regain his liberty, though on what terms, and how he was finally freed, the record does not say.

Keil and Delitzsch, whose comment runs on into the next verse, take the opportunity to explain a puzzle of arrangement: why the death of Jehoash, already noted at 2 Kings 13:12, should be recorded again here. In the annals of the kings of Israel, they reason, the notice stood after the account of this very war with Amaziah, and the historian gives it again as he found it, a judgment they support from the spelling of the royal name, which appears here in an older form than in the earlier closing notice. The witnesses do not divide. Together they complete the picture of Judah's humiliation: her God's house rifled of what little it held, her king's treasury emptied, her leading men led away as sureties, and the whole carried in triumph to Samaria. The plunder of the temple is the last and heaviest stroke, for it turns Amaziah's proud war into a sacrilege against the very sanctuary his good beginning had honoured, and seals the reversal that pride began.
15:
The closing formula for Jehoash returns, oddly, in the middle of Amaziah's story: "Now the rest of the acts of Jehoash which he did, and his might, and how he fought with Amaziah king of Judah, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel?" The reformed John Gill glosses "his might" as the valiant and mighty acts Jehoash performed in his wars with the Syrians, the recovery of Israel's cities that the previous chapter had promised and recorded. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, however, restricts the phrase "how he fought with Amaziah" to the one campaign already told: the only fighting between the two kings was the battle just narrated, set out more minutely in 2 Chronicles 25:21 and following.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry steps back to survey the stretch of verses now opening, in which three kings are brought to their graves. Jehoash of Israel is the first, and Henry pauses over the repetition, for "we attended his funeral once before," at 2 Kings 13:12; because the historian had further occasion to speak of his life and actions, chiefly his war with Amaziah, he mentions the death and burial a second time. Henry looks ahead to the other two: Amaziah, who would survive his conqueror fifteen years yet die by his subjects' hands, and Azariah, who would succeed only after a long interval in which the government lay with protectors. The whole passage, he sees, is a gathering-in of reigns, the sacred writer tidying his record before pressing on.

The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the formula is no idle repetition but the historian's device for closing a reign he had left open in order to tell the war with Judah, deferring the martial details, as ever, to the royal annals while Scripture keeps its own eye on what matters most. Gill's "might" looks back to Jehoash's Syrian victories, Clarke's to the single clash with Amaziah, and Henry's survey sets both within the larger movement of the chapter, where king after king is measured, buried, and succeeded. The reader is reminded that the mightiest exploits of these princes are recorded elsewhere, in books long lost; what Scripture preserves is not the full chronicle of their power but the moral weight of their reigns, and it is to that weight, the pride of Amaziah, the death of prophets, the mercy shown to Israel, that the narrative now returns.
16:
The death and burial of Jehoash of Israel are recorded in the settled formula of the book: "And Jehoash slept with his fathers, and was buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel; and Jeroboam his son reigned in his stead." The reformed John Gill glosses the opening idiom in his usual brief way, Jehoash "died as they died," going the common way of his fathers, the phrase "slept with his fathers" marking the ordinary end shared by kings good and bad alike.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to the burial and to the smooth succession it introduces. Jehoash was laid in Samaria among the kings of Israel, and his son Jeroboam, the second of that name, took his throne, so that the dynasty of Jehu passes to its fourth generation without a tremor. But their deeper interest is in what this notice makes room for. By closing out Jehoash's reign here, the historian frees himself to record that Amaziah of Judah lived on fifteen years after the death of his Israelite conqueror, and then to turn to the dark end of that Judean king, the conspiracy that would drive him from Jerusalem to Lachish and death, which the following verses relate. Keil and Delitzsch note that the earlier commentators sought the cause of that conspiracy in the humiliation of the war with Jehoash, but they object that the plot did not break out until fifteen years or more after the defeat, and point rather to the Chronicler's explanation at 2 Chronicles 25:27, that it was "from the time that Amaziah departed from the LORD" that men began to conspire against him.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill's plain "died as they died" and Keil and Delitzsch's careful placing of the notice meet in a single observation about the historian's method: he buries Jehoash out of Israel's annals at the point where those annals set his death, after the war with Judah, and uses the closing of the northern king's reign as a hinge on which to swing back to the fate of the southern one. The verse thus does double duty, sealing one reign and opening the way to the undoing of another. Behind the quiet succession of Jeroboam to his father's throne, and the peaceful "sleep" of Jehoash among his royal fathers, the narrative is already reaching toward the violent death that awaits the prouder and, on paper, the more righteous king of Judah, a contrast the chapter never lets the reader forget.
17:
A single chronological note carries a surprising weight: "And Amaziah the son of Joash king of Judah lived after the death of Jehoash son of Jehoahaz king of Israel fifteen years." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott shows how neatly the figure fits the surrounding data. Amaziah came to the throne in the second year of Jehoash, who reigned sixteen years (2 Kings 13:10), and reigned twenty-nine years in all (2 Kings 14:2); the fourteen years contemporary with Jehoash and the fifteen years after his death together make up the total, so that the different notices are perfectly self-consistent. Ellicott infers that Jehoash must have died very soon after his victory at Beth-shemesh, perhaps in the very next year. The reformed John Gill reckons the same arithmetic, fifteen years surviving plus fourteen contemporary equalling the twenty-nine of his reign, and records that the Vulgate here reads "twenty-five years" instead of fifteen, a variant he lets stand as a curiosity of the versions.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the human lesson the bare number conceals. Fifteen years Amaziah survived the conqueror who had humbled him, a long time to live under the shadow of a public disgrace. "A man may live a great while after he has been shamed," Henry observes, "may be thoroughly mortified, as Amaziah no doubt was, and yet not dead." The defeat did not kill him; it left him to a slow, diminished existence, his walls breached, his treasury emptied, his leading men taken as hostages, his pride broken but his life prolonged. There is a mercy in that reprieve, but also a severity: the king is made to live on among the ruins of his own presumption.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott's careful reconciliation of the figures and Gill's confirming tally establish that the chronology holds together without strain, while Henry's reflection supplies the reason the historian bothers to record so exact a span. Those fifteen years are not empty. They are the interval in which a humiliated king lives out the consequences of his folly, until the disaffection his defeat had bred ripens at last into the conspiracy that ends him. The verse, dry as it looks, measures the long patience of judgment, how a proud man, once broken, may be left for years to consider what his pride has cost, before the final reckoning falls.
18:
The reign of Amaziah is dismissed to the royal annals with the book's standing formula: "And the rest of the acts of Amaziah, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?" The reformed John Gill is careful to distinguish the source here named from the canonical book of Chronicles. Some things concerning Amaziah are indeed recorded in the inspired Chronicles that are not given here, and some things here are not there; but the "book of the chronicles" to which the historian refers is neither, it is the official annals of each reign, kept by the king's appointed historian, a court record now long lost.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds a pointed silence in the very wording of the formula. Amaziah's acts are said to be written in his annals, but, unlike the closing notices of many kings, nothing is said here of his might. And that omission, Henry judges, is deserved: "his cruelty when he was a conqueror over the Edomites," casting his captives headlong from the rock, as v.7 recorded, "and his insolence when he challenged the king of Israel" at v.8 "showed him void of true courage." A man may win battles and issue challenges and still be, at bottom, no brave man; cruelty and bluster are not valour, and the historian, by passing over Amaziah's "might" in silence, quietly withholds from him an honour he had not earned.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill's clarification of the source and Henry's reading of the omission meet in a single sober judgment on the reign. The formula that seems merely to defer the details to another book in fact renders a verdict by what it leaves out. Where the annals of a truly mighty king might boast of his prowess, Amaziah's record is allowed to close without that word, for his conquests had been marked by cruelty and his one great enterprise by a pride that ended in rout and captivity. So even this bare cross-reference to the lost chronicles carries the chapter's moral: that a reign is weighed not by the space it fills in the annals but by the character it reveals, and that Amaziah, measured so, is found wanting.
19:
The reign that began so well ends in blood: "Now they made a conspiracy against him in Jerusalem: and he fled to Lachish; but they sent after him to Lachish, and slew him there." The witnesses probe both the conspirators and their cause. The reformed John Gill supposes the plotters to have been the principal men of Jerusalem, perhaps those whose sons Jehoash had carried off as hostages, and who laid at Amaziah's door the breaching of the city wall and the pillaging of temple and palace that his folly had brought on. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agrees that the defeat and its consequences, the plundered temple, the emptied treasury, the dismantled walls, had made the king so unpopular that the whole of his last fifteen years was probably a series of troubles ending in this violent death.

The grammatical-historical witnesses press for a more exact cause, and here a real difference opens. Keil and Delitzsch object to laying the conspiracy at the door of the war with Jehoash, for the plot did not break out until fifteen years or more after that defeat; they prefer the Chronicler's account, that it was "from the time that Amaziah departed from the LORD," his apostasy in worshipping the gods of Edom, that men began to conspire against him, as 2 Chronicles 25:27 reports. Charles Ellicott reads the very anonymity of the record as significant: no individual conspirators are named, which points to a general disaffection rather than a private feud, and both the king's flight and the pursuit to Lachish suggest either a popular rising in the capital or a military revolt. Lachish itself, Ellicott notes, was an ancient and strong fortress, the modern Um Lakis, of the kind mentioned at 2 Chronicles 11:9; that Amaziah fled to it, and that his enemies still reached him there, shows how wide the disaffection ran.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sums the tragedy without softening it: Amaziah "was slain by his own subjects, who hated him for his maladministration," the breach in their walls having made Jerusalem too hot for him, so that he fled, and was at last murdered in his refuge. The witnesses thus divide over the precise spring of the plot, the lingering shame of the defeat, the guilt of the king's apostasy, or the broad resentment of his misrule, but they converge on the shape of the end. The king who had once obeyed the law of Moses against slaying the children of murderers now falls himself to the conspirator's sword; the man who had defied a rival to look him in the face dies fleeing, hunted from his own city to a distant fortress. Judgment, long deferred through fifteen quiet years, arrives at last, and the reign closes exactly opposite to the way it opened.
20:
The murdered king is carried home for burial: "And they brought him on horses: and he was buried at Jerusalem with his fathers in the city of David." The reformed John Gill takes the horses to mean a chariot or hearse drawn by them, but records the curious rabbinic fancy attached to the phrase: that Amaziah was carried on horseback, and this because he had worshipped the gods of the Edomites, who were themselves borne on horses; and that he was not carried on the shoulders of men, as the honoured dead might be, because he had neglected to serve the God of Israel, whose sacred things were carried on the shoulders. The tradition turns the manner of the burial into a quiet rebuke of the king's idolatry.

The grammatical-historical witnesses read the scene more soberly, and find in its very orderliness a clue to the nature of the crime. Charles Ellicott renders "they carried him upon the horses" and suggests either that the body was borne in the royal chariot in which Amaziah had fled from Jerusalem, or that the corpse was literally carried on horseback by the regicides themselves. What matters, he argues, is the decorum of the proceeding: the measured conveyance of the body, the burial of the king in the royal sepulchres with his fathers, and the immediate elevation of his son Azariah together prove that the murder was no act of private blood-revenge, but a public deed of state. Keil and Delitzsch concur, explaining that "they lifted him upon the horses" means upon the hearse to which the king's own horses had been harnessed, and that he was brought to Jerusalem and laid in the royal tomb.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill's homely tradition and the grammatical-historical reading of the funeral's decorum meet in a single impression: that even a king struck down by conspiracy is granted the outward honours of burial among his fathers in the city of David. The conspirators, whoever they were, did not fall upon the throne itself; they removed the man and at once set his son in his place, then buried the dead king with all the dignity his office required. There is a mercy in it, and a warning. The reign that ended in flight and slaughter is closed with the forms of royal honour, yet the tradition Gill preserves will not let the reader forget why it ended so, that a king who bowed to foreign gods was carried to his grave, men said, as those gods were carried, and not as the servants of the LORD.
21:
The people move at once to fill the empty throne: "And all the people of Judah took Azariah, which was sixteen years old, and made him king instead of his father Amaziah." The witnesses first settle the young king's two names. The reformed John Gill, the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, and John Wesley all note that this Azariah is the Uzziah of the following chapters and of 2 Chronicles 26:1, the two names differing little in sense, the one meaning "the help of the LORD," the other "the strength of the LORD." Keil and Delitzsch, weighing the matter more closely, judge the variation too constant across the books to be a mere copyist's error, and so take it as a genuine double name rather than a slip of the pen.

On the phrase "all the people of Judah" the grammatical-historical witnesses divide. Keil and Delitzsch insist it means the whole nation, not merely the men of war, and cite the popular acclamation as evidence that the succession was a national act. Charles Ellicott reports the contrary reading of Thenius, who took "all the people" to mean the army, as the same phrase arguably does at 2 Kings 13:7. Ellicott himself finds a further hint in the word "took": it seems to imply that Azariah was not the eldest son, for Amaziah, being fifty-nine at his death, had probably older sons than this boy of sixteen; Azariah was therefore chosen, Ellicott suggests, as a popular or perhaps a military favourite, and may have taken a new name on his accession, as Assyrian custom sometimes dictated when a sovereign was invested.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds a chronological caution that complicates the smooth picture. Though the boy was proclaimed king now, his full and independent reign, Wesley says, did not begin until twelve years after his father's death; for so long the government lay in the hands of protectors. The witnesses thus differ over who did the choosing, the whole nation or the army, and Ellicott and Wesley together qualify the appearance of an orderly, immediate succession: a younger son raised up over his elders, a favourite of people or soldiery, a boy set on the throne while others in fact ruled. Yet none of them doubts the main thing the verse asserts against the darkness of the conspiracy: that the house of David did not fall with Amaziah. The murdered king's line continues in his son; the crown passes, whatever the irregularities, from father to child; and the covenant with David, so often invoked in these chapters, holds firm even across a regicide.
22:
Before the reign of the new king is formally opened, the historian records one striking deed: "He built Elath, and restored it to Judah, after that the king slept with his fathers." The reformed John Gill sets the port in its long history of contested possession, Elath had belonged to Edom, was very probably taken by David when he made the Edomites tributary, was retaken by them when they revolted, and was won back by Amaziah in his recent campaign; now his son rebuilt and fortified it. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke locates it precisely, on the eastern branch of the Red Sea, the Elanitic Gulf that took its name from the town, and notes that Uzziah, having probably found it damaged in the late war, rebuilt it and brought it entirely under the dominion of Judah.

The grammatical-historical witnesses draw out the emphasis and the timing. Charles Ellicott observes that the pronoun "he" is emphatic, it was Uzziah, in contrast with his father, who did this; for Amaziah, weakened by his disastrous defeat, had perhaps not vigorously prosecuted the conquest of Edom, and may even have suffered further losses there, which Ellicott suggests could have contributed to the discontent that cost him his throne. The warlike youth, by contrast, took the field at once and pushed his arms to the southern extremity of Edom, restoring the state of things that had existed under Solomon and Jehoshaphat. Keil and Delitzsch agree that Uzziah "built," that is fortified, this Idumaean port, the same Elath of 1 Kings 9:26, and completed the renewed subjugation of Edom that his father had only begun. They explain the odd placement of the notice, set here before the account of the reign's length and character, by the importance of the work itself, which marked the vigorous commencement of the new king's rule and gave early proof of its power.

The witnesses do not divide, though they weigh the contrast between father and son. The clause "after that the king slept with his fathers," Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch agree, means immediately after the murder of Amaziah, a phrase that still surprises them by its position, but which underscores how promptly Uzziah acted. Where Amaziah's reign had ended in defeat, flight, and assassination, his son's opens with conquest and construction, a port rebuilt and a border restored. The single verse, brief and oddly placed, serves as a hinge between the ruin of the one reign and the vigour of the next, and quietly signals that the humiliation of Judah under Amaziah is already beginning to be reversed by the young king the people had set in his place.
23:
The narrative now turns north to the long reign of Jeroboam the second: "In the fifteenth year of Amaziah the son of Joash king of Judah, Jeroboam the son of Joash king of Israel began to reign in Samaria, and reigned forty and one years." The reformed John Gill notes only that this makes some fourteen or fifteen years of Jeroboam's reign contemporary with Amaziah, who reigned twenty-nine in all. But the number "forty and one" sets the grammatical-historical witnesses a problem. Keil and Delitzsch grant that the fifteenth-year synchronism agrees with v.17, but observe that a forty-one-year reign does not square with 2 Kings 15:8, where Jeroboam's son Zachariah does not come to the throne until the thirty-eighth year of Uzziah; and so Thenius would emend the figure to fifty-one, Ewald to fifty-three. Charles Ellicott reaches the same conclusion independently: reckoning fourteen years concurrent with Amaziah and thirty-seven with Azariah, he makes the true total fifty-one, and traces the received "forty-one" to a confusion of the Hebrew letters that stand for the two numbers. The witnesses thus divide over the correct length of the reign, some emending to fifty-one and one to fifty-three, all agreeing that the transmitted forty-one cannot be reconciled with the later synchronism.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry leaves the arithmetic to draw his lessons from the reign's length and quality. He suspects it a sign of the house of Jehu's stubborn attachment to the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat that they should name an heir-apparent by that infamous name at all. Jeroboam's reign was the longest of all the kings of Israel, forty-one years, yet his Judean contemporary Azariah reigned longer still, fifty-two; and he reigned just as long as the good king Asa had done at 1 Kings 15:10, though the one did evil and the other good. From this Henry draws a caution against reading prosperity as a mark of favour: "we cannot measure men's characters by the length of their lives or by their outward prosperity," for there is one event to the righteous and to the wicked.

Keil and Delitzsch supply the theological balance the chapter will develop: Jeroboam, like his fathers, held fast to the image-worship of the calves, and yet he raised his kingdom again to great power. The witnesses do not finally divide on the meaning, only on the number. They agree that a long and prosperous reign was granted to a king who never departed from Israel's founding sin, a paradox that the following verses will resolve not by crediting Jeroboam's piety, which was nil, but by appealing to the compassion of God for an afflicted people and to his covenant not yet withdrawn.
24:
The verdict on Jeroboam is the familiar northern refrain: "And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD: he departed not from all the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin." The reformed John Gill glosses the "evil" in a word, he was guilty of idolatry, keeping up the calf-worship that had been the state religion of the ten tribes since the first Jeroboam set it up. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the monotony of the charge: this king's character was the same as that of all the rest, for he departed not from the sins of his namesake but maintained the worship of the calves, thinking, no doubt, that there was no harm in it because it had been the way of all his ancestors and predecessors. Yet a sin, Henry insists, is never the less evil in God's sight for being an ancient usage, "and a frivolous plea it will be against doing good, that we have been accustomed to do evil."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record the same adherence to the image-worship, but immediately set beside it the great work the next verse will describe. Precisely this king, evil as he was, "brought back," that is restored, the boundary of Israel from the approach to Hamath in the north down to the sea of the Arabah, the Dead Sea, in the south, extending the kingdom again to the reach it had known in the days of Solomon at 1 Kings 8:65, and doing it "according to the word of the LORD" spoken by the prophet Jonah. The juxtaposition is deliberate and jarring: the verdict of unbroken evil here stands directly against the record of unprecedented success in the next.

The witnesses do not divide, and their agreement frames the theological puzzle the passage exists to raise. Gill and Henry fix the guilt, a persistent, inherited idolatry excused by custom, while Keil and Delitzsch point to the prosperity God nonetheless granted. How can a king who "departed not from all the sins of Jeroboam" be the very one under whom the LORD restores Israel's ancient borders? The verse states the evil baldly and leaves the tension unresolved, so that the reader is driven forward to v.26 and v.27, where the answer is given: not in Jeroboam's deserving, but in the compassion of God for an afflicted people he had not yet decreed to destroy.
25:
The evil king becomes the instrument of a great deliverance: "He restored the coast of Israel from the entering of Hamath unto the sea of the plain, according to the word of the LORD God of Israel, which he spake by the hand of his servant Jonah, the son of Amittai, the prophet, which was of Gath-hepher." The reformed John Gill reads it simply as the recovery of the border cities that Israel's enemies had seized, while the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke measures the scale of the reconquest: Jeroboam recovered all the territory that had been stripped from the kings of Israel, leaving the kingdom as ample as it had been when the ten tribes first separated under the first Jeroboam.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott maps the restored frontier with care. "From the entering of Hamath" marks the ideal northern boundary of Israel, fixed from the first and named again by the prophets, which the land had reached in its fullness only under Solomon; "the sea of the plain" is the Dead Sea, so that the whole length of the country was regained, much as the same limits are specified at Amos 6:14. Of the prophet through whom this was promised, Ellicott notes that he is the Jonah of Jonah 1:1, whose home Gath-hepher lay in Galilee, the modern Meshed not far north of Nazareth, as Joshua 19:13 places it. Keil and Delitzsch, whose comment carries over from the previous verse, add that Jeroboam brought the boundary back to the reach it had in Solomon's day, and that the prophet had probably framed his announcement in the very language of the Pentateuch, from which the phrase describing the southern border is drawn.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley identifies "the sea" as the Dead Sea, once the goodly plain of Genesis 13:10 before its overthrow, and now Israel's southern limit. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the eye from the map to the prophet, marvelling that God raised up Jonah the son of Amittai, a Galilean, "so much were those mistaken that said, Out of Galilee ariseth no prophet," as they would object at John 7:52, and by him intimated purposes of favour to Israel despite their provocations, encouraging the kingdom to take up arms for the recovery of its ancient possessions and assuring them of victory. It is a sign, Henry says, that God has not cast off his people when he continues faithful ministers among them: when Elisha, who had strengthened the hand of Joash, was gone, Jonah was sent to encourage his son. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the recovery was real, sweeping, and precisely as wide as the prophet had foretold, from Hamath to the Dead Sea, the borders of Solomon's day regained under a king who worshipped calves, a mercy the next verses will explain not by Jeroboam's worth but by God's compassion.
26:
Now the historian gives the reason behind the mercy of the preceding verses: "For the LORD saw the affliction of Israel, that it was very bitter: for there was not any shut up, nor any left, nor any helper for Israel." The reformed John Gill reads it as the ground of God's compassion, Israel was so sorely oppressed by her enemies, chiefly the Syrians, that the LORD was moved to pity, for the nation had been reduced to the most forlorn and helpless condition, with none shut up, none left, and no helper. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees that it was the bitterness of the affliction that moved God to pity and help a people otherwise unworthy, adding that both town and country had been utterly laid waste.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch locate the "higher ground" of Israel's revival squarely in the divine compassion: the LORD saw the great oppression and helpless condition of his people, and had not yet pronounced the decree of their rejection, and therefore sent help by the hand of Jeroboam. They hear in "not any shut up, nor any left" a deliberate echo of Moses' song at Deuteronomy 32:36, marking that Israel had been brought to the very extremity of distress there foretold, at which the LORD would interpose lest his people wholly perish. Charles Ellicott sharpens the vocabulary, preferring "oppression" to "affliction" and reading the word rendered "bitter" as rather "stubborn" or inveterate, an unyielding, enduring distress, and he compares the phrase "none shut up nor left" with its use at 1 Kings 14:10.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry dwells on the tenderness the verse ascribes to God. Their distress being very great made them the objects of his compassion; though he saw no signs of their repentance and reformation, yet he saw their affliction, that it was very bitter, and was moved by it. Henry points to the Scriptures that speak of God's "bowels of mercy," the yearning compassion of Jeremiah 31:20 and the LORD who is "full of compassion" in Psalms 86:15. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fills in the history, Israel had been so greatly reduced that the hand of God lay heavy even on the captive and the diseased, and there was no helper, until God sent Jonah to encourage them and promise better days. The witnesses do not divide, though they render the key word variously, "bitter" with Gill and Wesley, "stubborn" or inveterate with Ellicott. They converge on the astonishing motive the verse discloses: that the LORD's help came not because Israel had turned to him, for Henry is careful that no repentance is mentioned, nor because Jeroboam deserved it, but purely because God saw an oppression too bitter to leave unrelieved. It is compassion, not reward, that moves the deliverance.
27:
The deliverance is grounded, finally, in what God had not yet said: "And the LORD said not that he would blot out the name of Israel from under heaven: but he saved them by the hand of Jeroboam the son of Joash." The reformed John Gill reads the negative precisely, God had not as yet declared by any of his prophets that he would blot out Israel, though he would afterward say it by the prophet Hosea, and would fulfil it in the reign of Hoshea, when the northern kingdom fell and its people were carried away, as 2 Kings 17:6 records. The sentence of national death was real but not yet pronounced; and because it was not yet spoken, it was not yet executed.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms that "said not" means "by any prophet," and unfolds the metaphor: to "blot out the name" is a figure taken from the blotting out of writing, the more vivid because the Hebrews used inks that soon faded and could be wiped from the parchment, as the like image appears at Numbers 5:23. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley makes the same point in a breath, "not yet; he had not yet declared this, as afterwards he did by the succeeding prophets." Keil and Delitzsch, carrying their comment forward, add that the LORD saved Israel by Jeroboam's hand precisely because the decree of rejection had not gone out, and that the king's recovery even reached to Damascus and Hamath, of which the next verse speaks.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reasons closely from the wording. The decree for Israel's utter destruction had not yet gone forth; "he had not as yet said he would blot out the name of Israel, and because he had not said it he would not do it." Henry allows that if this be understood of the dispersion of the ten tribes, God did afterward both say it and do it; yet the name of Israel still remains under heaven, he insists, in the gospel Israel, and will to the end of time. For the present, because they still bore that honoured name which was to have so lasting a dignity, God showed them this favour, for the sake, too, of the ancient covenant honour of that name he had pledged to remember at 2 Kings 13:23. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Israel's survival hangs on a mercy expressed as restraint: the LORD withholds the word of doom, and in that withholding saves a guilty people by the hand of an idolatrous king. It is deliverance granted not because Israel is worthy, but because the appointed hour of judgment has not yet struck, a reprieve, the whole passage implies, and not an acquittal.
28:
The record of Jeroboam's reign closes with a notice of his conquests, and one famously obscure phrase: "Now the rest of the acts of Jeroboam, and all that he did, and his might, how he warred, and how he recovered Damascus, and Hamath, which belonged to Judah, for Israel, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel?" The reformed John Gill passes over it lightly as the king's valiant acts and warlike exploits. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the history behind the boast: David had conquered all Syria and set garrisons in Damascus, as 1 Chronicles 18:3 and following relate, but the Syrians had since thrown off that yoke and seized a portion of Israel's own territory; Jeroboam now recovered these, so that the places which had anciently belonged to Judah by David's conquests, and had been repossessed by Syria, he conquered and added to Israel.

The grammatical-historical witnesses set the victory in its wider world and then wrestle with the crux. Charles Ellicott dates Jeroboam as a contemporary of the Assyrian king Ramman-nirari, who exacted tribute from Tyre, Sidon, "the land of Omri," Edom, and Philistia, and besieged Damascus until its king submitted; it was this prostration of Israel's great enemy by Assyria, he argues, that made Jeroboam's own successes possible, he being himself in effect a vassal of the northern empire. Ellicott takes "recovered" in the sense of "restored," as at 2 Chronicles 8:3, and understands Damascus and Hamath to mean not the whole independent states but the portions of their territory that had belonged to Israel in Solomon's day.

Over the tangled clause "which belonged to Judah, for Israel" the two grammatical-historical readings diverge. Keil and Delitzsch take it to mean whatever in the two kingdoms of Damascus and Hamath had formerly belonged to Judah in the times of David and Solomon, now subjugated again to Israel, reading Damascus and Hamath as kingdoms, not mere cities. Ellicott treats "which belonged to Judah" rather as a restrictive epithet, "Judaean Damascus and Hamath," but confesses the phrase obscure and the text possibly corrupt, noting that Ewald would simply cancel the words "which belonged to Judah" and read "to Israel," while no proposed explanation is really satisfactory. The witnesses thus divide over how to construe the difficult notice, though they agree on the substance: that Jeroboam, under the shadow of a distracted Assyria, extended Israel's dominion to a reach unknown since Solomon. What the historian records without comment, and what the previous verses have taught the reader to see, is that this widest of Israel's recoveries fell to a king who never left the sin of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, a greatness of empire granted, once more, not as a reward but as the outworking of God's undeclared patience with his people.
29:
The long reign ends in the peace its victims never knew: "And Jeroboam slept with his fathers, even with the kings of Israel; and Zachariah his son reigned in his stead." The reformed John Gill notes only that the king died and was buried with his royal fathers. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke marks that Jeroboam "died a natural death," no small thing in the blood-stained annals of the north, and was regularly succeeded by his son Zachariah, though that son would reign badly and briefly, slain after six months by Shallum, who reigned but a month before Menahem cut him down in turn. Clarke recalls too that the prophet Amos lived in this reign, and was falsely accused by Amaziah, the idolatrous priest of Bethel, of having predicted that Jeroboam would die by the sword, a slander, for what Amos actually foretold may be read at Amos 7:10 and following.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to a chronological gap the verse conceals. Since Jeroboam reigned forty-one years, his death fell in the twenty-seventh year of Uzziah; yet his son Zachariah did not come to the throne until the thirty-eighth year of Uzziah, according to 2 Kings 15:8, so that he cannot have succeeded immediately upon his father's death. An interregnum of some years intervened, a lapse Clarke too remarks, confessing it "cannot be accounted for in a satisfactory manner." The smooth formula "his son reigned in his stead" masks a disordered succession and the swift descent into anarchy that the next chapter will record.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley lifts the closing notice into its place in the history of revelation. It was in the reign of this Jeroboam, he observes, that Hosea began to prophesy, the first to commit his prophecies to writing; at the same time Amos prophesied, soon after Micah, and then Isaiah in the days of Ahaz and Hezekiah. "Thus God never left himself without witness, but in the darkest ages of the church raised up some to be burning and shining lights, to their own age by their preaching and living, and a few by their writings to reflect light upon us, on whom the ends of the world are come." The witnesses do not divide. They close the chapter, and the reign, on a double note: the death of a mighty but unreformed king, whose dynasty will soon collapse into anarchy, and the rising of the writing prophets, through whom the LORD who had spared Israel by Jonah's word would now begin to record, for every later age, both his patience and the judgment that patience could not forever postpone.
